name: KPV
summary: KPV is a three-amino-acid C-terminal fragment of alpha-MSH studied for broad anti-inflammatory effects, particularly in gut and skin inflammation, without the pigmentary activity of the parent hormone.
mechanism:
KPV (lysine-proline-valine) is derived from positions 11-13 of alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (alpha-MSH) but lacks the melanocortin-receptor-binding region, so it produces no tanning or pigmentation. Its anti-inflammatory action is largely receptor-independent: after entering cells it inhibits NF-kB signaling by stabilizing IkB-alpha and blocking nuclear translocation of the p65 (RelA) NF-kB subunit, competing at the importin-alpha binding site. This suppresses transcription of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as TNF-alpha, IL-1beta, and IL-6.

In the intestine, KPV is taken up by epithelial and immune cells through the di/tripeptide transporter PepT1, which is upregulated during inflammation, allowing inflamed tissue to concentrate the peptide. In animal colitis models (DSS- and TNBS-induced), oral KPV reduced weight loss, colonic myeloperoxidase activity, histological damage, and inflammatory cytokine expression at nanomolar concentrations.
benefits: Reduces NF-kB-driven inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-alpha, IL-1beta, IL-6)
May support gut/intestinal inflammation (preclinical colitis models)
No melanocortin-receptor activity, so no skin tanning or pigmentation
Self-targets inflamed intestinal tissue via upregulated PepT1 transporter
risks: Human clinical evidence is limited; most data is preclinical (animal/cell studies)
Not FDA-approved; sourcing and purity vary by supplier
Long-term safety in humans is not established

dosage.notes: Typically 200-500 mcg/day. Oral capsules favored for gut/IBD (PepT1 self-targeting); SubQ used for systemic effect; topical for skin. Doses above 500 mcg are not better supported by evidence.
dosage.timing: Once daily, commonly in the morning; some protocols split into two smaller doses (AM/PM) for systemic anti-inflammatory effect.
dosage.maxDose: Community ceiling ~500 mcg/day for SubQ use; doses above this are not supported by evidence and offer no clear added benefit. No clinically validated human maximum exists.

steps.note: Once daily; start low to assess tolerance.
Once daily if well tolerated. Human protocols are anecdotal and not clinically validated; most evidence is preclinical (animal/cell models).

cycleLength: Commonly used in 4-8 week cycles, or as-needed for acute flares; no formal human protocol established

reconstitution.notes: A 10 mg vial in 2 mL bacteriostatic water yields 5 mg/mL (5000 mcg/mL); 0.05 mL = 250 mcg. Oral/topical formulations are also common and bypass injection.

storage: Store lyophilized vial in freezer; refrigerate (2-8 C) after reconstitution and use within ~2-4 weeks; protect from light.
contraindications: Active malignancy (use only under medical guidance given limited data)

interactions.note: Frequently combined for gut healing and anti-inflammatory support; complementary mechanisms (anecdotal/preclinical).
Additive immunomodulation is theoretically possible; use under medical supervision.

references.title: Dalmasso et al. PepT1-Mediated Tripeptide KPV Uptake Reduces Intestinal Inflammation (Gastroenterology, 2008)
